title: Sorting properties and selection

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Sorting properties and selection; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Sorting properties and selection
sources.claim: Sorting choice follows data size, stability, memory, key cost, existing order, worst-case requirements, and language library guarantees.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Sorting choice follows data size, stability, memory, key cost, existing order, worst-case requirements, and language library guarantees.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Stable sorting preserves original order among equal scores when earlier registration order is meaningful. Your goal is to select a sorting strategy for a real requirement and justify algorithm versus library use. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Arrays, strings and matrices; Time, space and asymptotic growth; Merge sort and quicksort. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Sorting choice follows data size, stability, memory, key cost, existing order, worst-case requirements, and language library guarantees. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Handwritten algorithms rarely beat mature libraries, and external or parallel data adds different constraints. The draft therefore rejects: The asymptotically fastest named sort is always the correct choice.

includes: Compare stability, adaptiveness, in-place behavior, bounds and library guarantees.
Select a sorting strategy for a real requirement and justify algorithm versus library use.

In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.
excludes: Handwritten algorithms rarely beat mature libraries, and external or parallel data adds different constraints.

outcomes: Select a sorting strategy for a real requirement and justify algorithm versus library use.

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Choose from required guarantees and use the standard library sort unless the problem specifically teaches or requires another algorithm.

examples.title: Sorting properties and selection: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, pass buffers and counts and use qsort/bsearch comparators carefully; in C++, prefer standard algorithms and strict comparators; in Java, use Arrays/Collections algorithms with comparator contracts; in Python, use sorted/list.sort and bisect with key and stability semantics.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Handwritten algorithms rarely beat mature libraries, and external or parallel data adds different constraints.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Sorting properties and selection, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
